migrationNotes:
Part 1 — Phase Composition: For each Phase, list which client cohorts are included, explaining what criteria drove the grouping; for all clients, specify which client is mapped to each Phase.  FIS has intentionally not prescribed a Phase structure or grouping methodology. We expect vendors to analyze the client dataset provided and propose the Phase design they believe is most operationally sound for an engagement of this complexity. There is no correct number of Phases. Responses that demonstrate genuine analytical thinking about client risk, regulatory complexity, volume sequencing, and transition readiness will be evaluated more favorably than generic frameworks.

Why Tier 3 - Phase 1: Lowest revenue and typically lower volume = lowest business exposure while the operating model is being established.

What it enables: Safe “pilot” cohort to validate people + process + tech + controls before scaling.

Service logic: Start with Off‑Phone & Accounting first to build and test overall infrastructure - operations and enabling

Why Tier 3 - Phase 2: Expands Tier 3 after Phase 1 learnings are implemented—stabilized playbooks and improved agent readiness.

What it enables: Controlled scaling—adds complexity gradually without increasing revenue exposure materially.

Service logic: Introduce Customer Service / Fraud / Disputes once foundational workflows and governance are stable.

Why Tier 3 - Phase 3: Adds higher operational sensitivity (e.g., Collections / Loyalty) only after early operational rhythm is proven.

What it enables: Lower-tier clients absorb the first “revenue-impacting” processes while still keeping risk contained.

Service logic: Layer Loyalty Program + Collections after voice operations show stable QA and compliance adherence.

Why Tier 3 -  Phase 4: Reserve highest complexity (e.g., Fraud Outbound and Dispute Resolution) for the final Phase—even within low revenue tiers—to avoid early failures in complex motion.
What it enables: Ensures outbound fraud approach, scripting, and compliance controls are mature before launch.
Service logic: Fraud Outbound and Dispute Resolution last because it has the highest execution complexity and reputational risk.

Dispute Resolution (Back Office including Fraud Investigations)

Why Tier 2 - Phase 1: Only Tier 2 clients enter Phase 1 when they are closest to standard or have clean data/process maturity—still manageable exposure.

What it enables: Early scale signal without taking on high-value risk; tests capacity planning and staffing model robustness.

Service logic: Off‑Phone & Accounting first to establish controls and reporting integrity before increasing customer-facing intensity.

Why Tier 2 -  Phase 2: Brings a broader Tier 2 set once Phase 1 is stable—less rework, faster onboarding, and better training outcomes.

What it enables: Confident ramp with measured complexity and improved SLA predictability.

Service logic: Add Customer Service / Fraud / Disputes after KPI tracking, QA calibration, and escalation paths are fully functional.

Why Tier 2 -  Phase 3: Introduces Collections + Loyalty after voice quality and dispute handling have stabilized—reduces revenue leakage and customer friction risks.

What it enables: Mid-revenue clients benefit from proven compliance, call strategy, and coaching routines.

Service logic: Collections is operationally and compliance sensitive, so it enters only after earlier Phases have proven call handling and control discipline.

Why Tier 2 -  Phase 4: Adds high-complexity / high-judgment motions last to protect mid-revenue accounts from early-stage variability.

What it enables: Fraud outbound operates on mature analytics, refined scripts, and stable QA—reducing false positives/negatives and customer complaints.

Service logic: Fraud Outbound last to ensure tooling, governance, and agent proficiency are proven.

Why Tier 1 - Phase 1: Low variance (simpler service mix, fewer exceptions) and readiness is high

What it enables: Early benefits for large revenue while still limiting scope to foundational services.

Service logic: Start with Off‑Phone only—creates clean reporting, billing accuracy, and operational backbone without heavy customer impact.

Why Tier 1 - Phase 2: Phase 1 stabilization reduces risk of service disruption for high revenue clients; playbooks are tested.

What it enables: Launch customer-facing services with confidence—lower complaint risk and better first-contact outcomes.

Service logic: Add Customer Service / Fraud / Disputes once quality and escalation governance is proven end-to-end.

Why Tier 1 -  Phase 3: Final Phase protects the highest non-dedicated revenue from early-stage risk in the most complex process.

What it enables: Fraud outbound and Dispute resolution performance is optimized before it touches highest-value dedicated clients—minimizes reputational and regulatory risk.

Service logic: Fraud Outbound and Dispute Resolution last due to complex decisioning, controls, and higher sensitivity.

Why Dedicated Clients - Phase 1: Limited to non-customer-facing, low-risk scope with strict readiness gates.

What it enables: Establishes baseline integrations and reporting without exposing customer experience.

Service logic: Off‑Phone & Accounting only; no complex real-time motion until model stability is proven.

Why Dedicated Clients -  Phase 2: If Phase 1 is stable, dedicated clients can begin controlled expansion—still phased and tightly governed.

What it enables: Early benefits while safeguarding experience through proven playbooks and stable tech.

Service logic: Introduce Customer Service / Fraud / Disputes only after success criteria are met (QA, AHT, escalation TAT, SLA stability).

Why Dedicated Clients -  Phase 3: Collections and Loyalty roll in after voice operations and dispute/fraud inbound are consistently stable.

What it enables: Protects high-visibility revenue from early compliance/quality variability.

Service logic: Collections + Loyalty only when audit readiness, call monitoring, and coaching cadence are steady state.

Why Dedicated Clients - Phase 4: Dedicated accounts get the most complex and highest-risk motion last to ensure the model is fully proven.

What it enables: Maximum risk reduction where it matters most—largest revenue, strictest expectations, highest scrutiny.

Service logic: Fraud Outbound and Dispute resolution last once tooling, decision controls, and governance have been validated across earlier Phases.

No EMEA clients’ migrations: BAU operations continue post-rebadging

Design principles to be further aligned and refined in partnership with FIS during solutioning exercise

Five clients considered out of scope due to zero projected revenue. (NA-1, NA-57, NA-35 Canada, NA-26, NA-58)
IVR excluded across all clients (part of Tech migration)

NA-2 excludes Phase 4 based on service scope is already being covered in Phase 3

evaluationBriefText:
EXL
Vendor Evaluation Brief
FIS TMS Managed Services RFP (TMS-RFP-2026-001)
March 30, 2026 | Confidential
Evaluation Tear Sheet
Total data points: 52
Executive Assessment Memo
EXL
Bottom Line
EXL proposes the lowest 5-year TCV in the field at $477M — driven by an aggressive India-centric offshoring model that moves 100% of NA operations to Pune, Noida, and Kochi within 9 months of rebadge, while retaining EMEA fully in-country. The resulting $274M in 5-year savings (vs standard baseline) exceeds FIS targets from FY27 onward by significant margins. However, the proposal carries material structural risks: severance costs are explicitly excluded and fall to FIS, COLA is exclusive and EMEA pricing is at existing cost +20% markup, CCaaS migration costs are carved out of the base price, and the proposal contains significant data gaps with the fixed fee schedule, technology governance tab, and several supporting sections incomplete or marked WIP. EXL delivers strong analytics capability through an existing FIS partnership but presents a less mature operational readiness picture than established BPO competitors in this field.
Underlying Assumptions in their Proposal
100% India delivery for NA: All NA operations transition to India (Pune/Noida for voice, Kochi for back office) within 9 months. Columbus GA, Sudbury, and Manila exit by Month 9. This is the most aggressive offshoring assumption in the vendor field. Voice delivery from India uses AI accent neutralization. (Proposal Part 1 p.15, p.17)
EMEA fully retained in-country with -29% labor arbitrage: EMEA stays at MK, Hamburg, Barneveld with no offshoring. The labor arbitrage is NEGATIVE (-29%) because rebadged employees cost more under EXL than under FIS. EXL’s EMEA savings come entirely from 43% AI/Digital — the highest AI assumption for any vendor in any geography. (Tab 9.0 row 8)
Severance excluded: “The Commercials have not baked in any severance cost and same shall be borne by FIS.” This is a material carve-out that other vendors absorb or at least share. TUPE, §613a BGB, and Art 7:662 all carry severance obligations. (Tab 6.0 Assumption #5)
CCaaS excluded: The base commercial proposal excludes NICE CXone migration costs. “In case customer requires the solution that will be additional.” This means the headline TCV of $477M does not include the technology migration that every other vendor prices in. (Tab 6.0 Assumption #3)
Strengths & Differentiators
Lowest TCV and aggressive Y2+ savings trajectory: At $477M 5-year TCV, EXL is ~5% below the next lowest vendor. FY27 savings of $57M and FY28 of $75M substantially exceed FIS targets. The Y3–Y5 steady-state cost of ~$74M/yr is the lowest annual run rate in the field. (Tab 6.0 row 112; Calculated vs targets)
Existing FIS analytics partnership: EXL already supports FIS’s CMO organization with Customer 360, lead scoring, MuleSoft integrations, and Salesforce migration. This embedded data and analytics capability is unique in the vendor field and could accelerate AI-driven transformation. (Proposal Part 2 p.7)
20-21% FTE reduction committed and guaranteed: Unlike other vendors who mark AI savings as “indicative” or “TBD,” EXL commits to a guaranteed 20-21% FTE reduction through process standardization and LC/NC automation, with minimal FIS dependency. The additional 24-25% AI upside requires “light-touch collaboration” from FIS. (Proposal Part 1 p.21)
Dual TOM options presented: EXL evaluated and transparently presented two operating models — Option 1 (rebadge then offshore) and Option 2 (Day-1 offshore, EMEA retained by FIS). This analytical rigor gives FIS optionality and shows EXL’s willingness to tailor the solution. (Proposal Part 1 p.12)
Key Risks & Concerns
Significant data gaps and incomplete submission: The fixed fee schedule (Tab 6.0 rows 53-55) is empty. The technology governance section (Tab 5.0) appears incomplete. Several tabs are marked WIP. The rate card provides offshore (India) rates only with no onshore pricing — EMEA is simply “existing cost + 20% markup” with no rate-level transparency. This limits the ability to independently validate the commercial model.
Severance + CCaaS + COLA exclusions distort the headline TCV: The $477M excludes severance (potentially $5-10M+ for TUPE/BGB/Art 7:662), CCaaS migration (other vendors include $5-18M), and annual COLA (4% India, actuals for EMEA). The true all-in 5-year cost is materially higher than stated. FIS must gross up EXL’s TCV to compare fairly against vendors who include these items.
100% India for NA voice carries CX and regulatory risk: Moving all NA voice to India within 9 months is operationally ambitious. AI accent neutralization is proposed to bridge the gap, but this technology is unproven at scale for regulated financial services. FDCPA collections from India raises jurisdictional compliance questions. No North American delivery presence post-Month 9 means zero geographic resilience for NA operations.
EMEA “retained in-country” at -29% labor arbitrage: EXL’s EMEA model actually costs FIS MORE for labor than the current state. The entire EMEA value proposition rests on 43% AI/Digital savings — the highest AI assumption any vendor has made for any geography. If AI underdelivers in EMEA, FIS is paying a premium with no fallback.
Key Questions to Pressure-Test in Workshop
Your fixed fee schedule is empty and your technology governance tab is incomplete. Can you provide the completed Tab 6.0 rows 53-55 and Tab 5.0 governance commitments before the workshop? Without these, scoring on Commercial Attractiveness and Tech Governance is not possible.
Gross up your TCV: What is the all-in 5-year cost including (a) estimated severance for TUPE/BGB/Art 7:662, (b) CCaaS migration, and (c) COLA at your stated rates? Provide this in a single comparable figure.
Your EMEA labor arbitrage is -29%. Walk us through the unit economics: what exactly does the +20% markup cover, and why does your EMEA model cost more than FIS’s current in-house operation for the same retained workforce?
100% India for NA voice: What is your accent neutralization technology, what is its accuracy rate in live financial services environments, and which specific clients (if any) have you deployed it with at scale? What is the BCP/DRP plan if the India sites experience disruption with no NA fallback?
You presented Option 2 as your recommendation but priced Option 1. Under Option 2, what is the all-in TCV, and how does the FIS-retained EMEA cost compare to the rebadge model?
Go-Forward Implications
EXL’s exclusion of severance, CCaaS, and COLA from the headline TCV highlights the need for a standardized cost comparison methodology. FIS should require every vendor to submit a “total cost of ownership” table that includes all transition costs, vendor fees, retained FIS costs, COLA, and technology migration — in a single comparable format.
The 43% AI/Digital assumption for EMEA (where there is no offshoring) provides a useful test case for pure AI-driven savings potential. If EXL can substantiate this with contractual commitment, it establishes a benchmark for what AI alone can deliver in an onshore-only, regulated European environment.
EXL’s existing analytics partnership with FIS is a genuine differentiator that could accelerate the data-led transformation. FIS should evaluate whether this embedded relationship can be leveraged regardless of the TMS vendor selection — potentially combining EXL’s analytics with another vendor’s operational delivery.

snippets.text:
EXL operates one of the most geographically diverse delivery networks in the industry with over 60,000+ professionals across 50+ global delivery centres, supporting services in 25+ languages. Our footprint is designed to give Banking & Capital Markets clients the flexibility to choose from onshore, nearshore, and offshore delivery models — or a blended combination — based on their cost, regulatory, and operational requirements.

EXL currently delivers contact centre and back-office services from the following 13 countries and 50+ sites ( all company managed)

Relevance for FIS engagement 
Our delivery model provides several structural advantages:

Regulatory alignment — Our onshore US and UK presence enables us to keep sensitive, regulated processes (e.g., KYC, AML, credit decisioning oversight) in-country where required, while routing high-volume, lower-risk processing to cost-optimal offshore locations.

Timezone coverage — Our combination of India, Philippines, South Africa, and the Americas enables 24/7 follow-the-sun operations without artificial hand-offs or service degradation at shift boundaries.

Data sovereignty and localisation — European clients benefit from our EU-based delivery in Bulgaria, Romania, Czech Republic, and Ireland, supporting GDPR-compliant data residency requirements. South African operations similarly support UK and European clients requiring nearshore English-language delivery within GDPR-aligned jurisdictions.

Resilience and BCP — No single-site dependency. Our multi-site India, Philippines, and South Africa configurations mean that any Banking & Capital Markets programme we support has built-in geographic redundancy and a documented business continuity failover plan.

Language capability — Across our global network we support 25+ languages, enabling multilingual contact centre operations for banking clients operating across diverse customer geographies.

We have recommended a programme-specific delivery design for FIS RFP recommending the optimal site mix based on your volume profile, language requirements, regulatory constraints, and cost objectives.

snippets.text:
EXL has built a strong and credible track record in serving card-issuing financial institutions over the past 20+ years, supporting both established banks and emerging fintech issuers across global markets including North America, EMEA, and APAC.

Scale & Client Footprint
Active Card Issuer Clients: 30+ financial institutions, including Tier 1 banks, mid-sized issuers, and fintech-led card programs  

Card Portfolios Supported: Credit cards, debit cards, prepaid cards, and commercial/corporate card programs

Commercial Scale : (ACV) Managed: USD 300M+ across card-related programs

Resource Base: 6,500+ FTEs dedicated to cards and payments operations

Transaction Volumes: Supporting 250M+ annual transactions across servicing, disputes, and back-office operations

Scope of Services Delivered:
End-to-end card lifecycle management:
Customer onboarding & KYC
Card activation & servicing (inbound/outbound)
Fraud detection support & transaction monitoring
Disputes & chargebacks (including compliance with card network rules)
Collections 
Back-office processing (reconciliations, settlement support, exception handling)

Performance & Delivery Milestones:

Managing Large scale migration :  Successfully scaled a large Custodian bank engagement from win mandate to 1000 + resources supporting customer service , payment Operations & other back office processes to multiple host locations at EXL in less than a year

STP Enablement: Achieved up to 85–90% straight-through processing in dispute intake and resolution workflows through automation and rules engines

Turnaround Time Improvements: Reduced dispute resolution TAT by 30–40% 

Productivity improvement : Delivered 30–50% efficiency savings through process optimization, digital transformation and global delivery leverage

Customer Experience: Improved CSAT/NPS scores by 100-150 bps across multiple issuer programs journey redesign and CX enabled digital interventions

Regulatory & Compliance Excellence: Zero material audit findings across major client engagements over the last 5 years

snippets.questionText: Describe your approach to managing sub-contractors used in delivering TMS services. Confirm: (1) whether sub-contractors would be used on this engagement, which subcontractors would be used and where work would be performed; (2) your SLA flow-down mechanism; (3) whether the client retains audit rights over sub-contractors.
snippets.text:
EXL's delivery model is centered around " the principle of Single accountability" to ensure governance cadence,  minimized operational and compliance risk and complete program ownership. We confirm

1. No subcontractors will be used for this engagement.
All services including Operations services and Technology led platform migrations ( CCAS migration) will be owned and delivered through our in-house teams, operating from secure and company-managed delivery centers.

2. Since EXL will be working on a "No subcontracting model"  the SLA flow-down to third parties is not applicable in this case. All Service Level Agreements (SLAs) will be directly owned and managed by our internal delivery and governance teams.

3. With a "No subcontracting " model in place , all of our services fall within EXL's direct operational control . EXL will extend complete access and full audit rights over our delivery centers, processes, controls, and resources involved in the engagement.

snippets.text:
Conversational / AI-based IVR

AWS Connect (in deployment)

AWS Connect

Phasing Strategy 

Considering AWS Connect is live.Proposed solution Avaya/AWS to NICE/Azure

Phase 1: Establish the technical core by replicating Amazon Lex V2 intents and AWS Lambda logic into Azure AI Agent Service and Azure Functions. Use the legacy Avaya AAEP to perform API "dips" into the new Azure brain, validating its ability to handle FIS/TSYS data in a live environment before migrating telephony.
Phase 2: Migrate telephony to NICE CXone while maintaining a multi-cloud posture via the Virtual Agent Hub (VAH). The VAH orchestrates traffic by routing legacy workflows to AWS Lex and new "Agentic" workflows to Azure.
Phase 3: Systematically decommission AWS by transitioning existing use cases. Systematically decommission AWS by transitioning existing use cases. But ensuring containment KPIs match the AWS baseline before the final cutover.
Phase 5: Design new use cases and deploy It will follow a fresh deployment cycle after building new workflows in agent co-pilot. Deploy for selected journeys (customer service), validate containment and CX
Expand across fraud, disputes, loyalty with continuous tuning

Proposed Sequencing

The proposed sequencing follows a Risk-Tiered Migration model, starting with low-complexity, informational flows to validate the Azure connection and speech-to-text accuracy. During the initial Due Diligence phase, all existing AWS Connect flows will be audited and assigned complexity scores based on their decision nodes and back-end API dependencies. Wave 1 will focus on low-risk "Canary" deployments, while subsequent waves will transition transactional and high-sensitivity flows—such as those for the Fraud team—only after technical validation is complete.

Migration Approach

The migration approach transitions legacy Avaya/AWS environments to a modern NICE CXone and Azure AI ecosystem through a structured four-phase plan. Phase 1 establishes the technical core by replicating Amazon Lex V2 intents and AWS Lambda logic into Azure AI Agent Service, using the legacy Avaya AAEP for API "dips" to validate FIS/TSYS data handling. Phase 2 migrates telephony to NICE CXone, utilizing the Virtual Agent Hub (VAH) to orchestrate traffic between legacy AWS Lex and new Azure "Agentic" workflows. Phase 3 systematically decommissions the AWS footprint via A/B testing, ensuring Azure’s containment KPIs match legacy baselines before final cutover. Finally, Phase 4 initiates a separate implementation cycle to design and deploy fresh Agent Co-pilot workflows, leveraging high-volume use case data to drive long-term AI transformation.

Architecture Pattern

The proposed architecture follows a Hybrid Cloud pattern, utilizing NICE CXone telephony as an orchestration layer and Microsoft Azure for Agentic conversational AI. At the core, the NICE Virtual Agent Hub (VAH) serves as a multi-cloud gateway, interfacing with legacy AWS Lex and new Azure AI Agent Service endpoints. By leveraging Event-Driven Microservices through Azure Functions, the architecture ensures that AI-driven insights automatically trigger downstream actions in Pega and maximizes containment.

Data Migration Approach

Identify and train high-volume intents using Transform.CX
Leverage existing knowledge base and FAQs
Store interaction data (voice/chat transcripts) for:
Continuous model training
Performance optimization
Enable API-driven real-time responses via backend systems

On contract award

Overall deployment timeline (8-9 months) after pre-requisites are meet.

The data migration approach follows a Phased Data Federation model, prioritizing Data Integrity and Zero-Downtime Synchronization between legacy and target environments. During the Due Diligence phase, metadata and lineage for all FIS/TSYS interaction logs are audited to establish a baseline without the risks associated with traditional bulk ETL.

Access for core business application and infrastructure.
Platform licensing and credits

snippets.questionText: Describe security controls for remote/WFH agents handling cardholder data (screen capture, device management, network security, print restrictions).
snippets.text:
EXL’s solution does not inherently include WFH capabilities. However, in circumstances where explicit approval is required for WFH, EXLfollows a structured process across its client engagements which is as follows:
1) Restrict remote/WFH agents’ access to cardholder data to secure Citrix/VDI or VM environments with segregated access, where application URLs are accessible only from within the Citrix/VDI network and users are authenticated via VPN and multi-factor authentication.
2) Prevents screen capture and unauthorized data movement, print screen and copy-paste functions are disabled, and endpoints are configured with OS-level restrictions that can disable screenshot capture and other local actions.
3) EXL enforces hardened endpoints with boot and disk layer encryption, restricted user profiles, no admin rights, and regular automated patching, along with endpoint security controls including NextGen Anti-Virus, EDR, URL filtering, Anti-APT, and DLP controls.
4)To reduce data exfiltration via removable media, USB and CD/DVD drives are disabled, modems/data cards are not permitted on production floors, and removable media is blocked by default on endpoints.
5) For network security, EXL uses NextGen firewalls with integrated IPS/Anti-APT at entry points, dedicated business VLANs with ACL-based logical segregation, NAC for endpoint posture validation, and remote connectivity via Palo Alto VPN solution (Global Protect) with full tunneling to ensure all traffic traverses the EXL network.
To enforce print restrictions, printing capabilities are disabled in offshore environments.

snippets.questionText: FCA regulated activities — describe your compliance approach for UK-regulated cardholder servicing including complaint handling.
snippets.text:
EXL UK is an FCA authorised entity and operates within a robust governance, risk management, and compliance monitoring framework designed to ensure adherence to all applicable UK regulatory requirements. As an outsourced service provider, EXL delivers cardholder servicing activities on behalf of its clients, supporting UK customers in line with client defined processes, FCA rules and contractual obligations. Our control environment is underpinned by:

1.	A structured three lines of defence model, ensuring independent oversight and challenge 
2.	Ongoing compliance monitoring and assurance programmes 
3.	Clearly defined policies and procedures aligned to the FCA handbook 

We maintain controls aligned to industry standards such as PCI DSS to ensure secure handling of cardholder data, alongside broader information security, and data protection frameworks. 

In relation to complaint handling, EXL UK supports clients by delivering complaint management services where required. All complaint handling activities are conducting in accordance with FCA DISP rules, including timeliness, fairness, and transparency requirements. Where EXL UK is not the final decision maker, we operate within defined escalation and hand off frameworks to ensure clients meet their regulatory obligations, including Financial Ombudsman Service requirements.  

This approach ensures that customer outcomes remain fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA expectations.

Our compliance model for collections, disputes, and customer service is anchored in strong governance, embedded controls, and independent oversight.

1. Governance and Accountability
Centralized governance with clear ownership, standardized workflows, and defined escalation protocols.

2. Training and First Line Compliance
Role-based compliance training supported by embedded first line controls, including regulatory alignment, license management, risk assessments, and checklist-driven control testing.

3. Quality Assurance (QA)
Ongoing QA reviews (call monitoring, case audits) to ensure adherence, with insights driving coaching and process improvements.

4. Internal Audit and Monitoring
Independent audits and continuous monitoring through dashboards and exception reporting to track compliance and drive remediation.

snippets.text: Yes, FIS will retain full ownership of its data at all times. EXL will not store any client owned data within its systems. Only operational data related to FIS, along with associated employee productivity metrics, will be maintained by EXL to support service delivery and performance management. This data will remain fully accessible to the client and will be shared upon request, including during transition or exit, strictly in accordance with agreed governance frameworks and data access protocols.
